summary: Prompted by SourceGraph's decision to abandon its Apache 2.0 license and go closed source, DeVault argues that contributor license agreements (CLAs) with copyright assignment clauses exist solely to let companies pull the rug on open source projects. He explains that CLAs give upstream maintainers more rights over contributions than contributors received, subverting the open source social contract. He finds SourceGraph's case especially egregious since their permissive license already allowed proprietary use — the CLA simply let them avoid even crediting contributors. He recommends copyleft licensing with collective copyright ownership as the strongest protection, and the Developer Certificate of Origin as an ethical alternative to CLAs for establishing provenance.
key_points: CLAs with copyright assignment exist to let companies relicense open source projects as proprietary software, subverting the social contract of open source
SourceGraph's case is particularly bad because their permissive Apache 2.0 license already allowed proprietary use — the CLA let them avoid even basic attribution obligations
Without a CLA, copyright is held collectively by all contributors, making relicensing impossible without unanimous consent
Copyleft licensing combined with no CLA is the strongest promise that a project will remain free software
The Developer Certificate of Origin handles the legitimate parts of a CLA (provenance and liability) without demanding copyright assignment
money_quotes: Once again we convene in the ritual condemnation we offer to commercial products that piss in the pool of open source.
This is a strategy employed by commercial companies with one purpose only: to place a rug under the project, so that they can pull at the first sign of a bad quarter.
This strategy exists to subvert the open source social contract.
The owners of SourceGraph find the simple task of crediting their contributors too onerous. This is disgusting.
Make no mistake: a CLA is a promise that a open source software project will one day become non-free. Don't sign them.
A CLA is a promise that software will one day become non-free; you can also promise the opposite.
It is not ethical to demand copyright assignment in addition to the free labor of the open source community.
key_insight: A CLA is fundamentally a trap door for relicensing — the combination of copyleft licensing and collective copyright ownership is the only reliable promise that software will remain free.
